title: Merging Queue Health (_internal)
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd group=queue name=*merge*
| eval fill_pct=if(max_size>0, round(current_size/max_size*100,1), null())
| where fill_pct > 70
| stats latest(fill_pct) as fill_pct, latest(current_size) as depth by host, name
| sort -fill_pct
description: Merging queue backlog delays structured indexing and can block hot buckets. Monitoring prevents silent pipeline stall on heavy merge workloads.
value: Merging queue backlog delays structured indexing and can block hot buckets. Monitoring prevents silent pipeline stall on heavy merge workloads.
implementation: Track merging-related queue names per indexer. Alert on high fill or rapid growth. Correlate with index volume, replication, and disk I/O.
visualization: Line chart (merge queue depth over time), Table (top merging queues).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd` `group=queue` (merging / agg queues).
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Track merging-related queue names per indexer. Alert on high fill or rapid growth. Correlate with index volume, replication, and disk I/O.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd group=queue name=*merge*
| eval fill_pct=if(max_size>0, round(current_size/max_size*100,1), null())
| where fill_pct > 70
| stats latest(fill_pct) as fill_pct, latest(current_size) as depth by host, name
| sort -fill_pct
```

Understanding this SPL

**Merging Queue Health (_internal)** ‚Äî Merging queue backlog delays structured indexing and can block hot buckets. Monitoring prevents silent pipeline stall on heavy merge workloads.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd` `group=queue` (merging / agg queues). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: splunkd. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=splunkd. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **fill_pct** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Filters the current rows with `where fill_pct > 70` ‚Äî typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, name** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Run the search over calm and busy windows. Compare with the Splunk monitoring console and relevant health and introspection views, and spot-check key fields in sample events.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (merge queue depth over time), Table (top merging queues).
grandmaExplanation: We watch how full our indexing and processing queues are so we can act before data stacks up, slows, or is dropped.
knownFalsePositives: Queues fill during indexing surges, search peaks, or after restart. We rule out maintenance, bundle work, and traffic spikes before we chase a single node.
